Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I juggle so many things. I'd block out chunks of time when I could, usually around 26 hours a week total, but it wasn't always consistent. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes and rewriting things in my own words. Sometimes I'd have really productive weeks where everything clicked, and other times my anxiety would spike and I'd struggle to focus even when I was sitting at my desk.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was coming up. Deadlines would push me into intense study sessions, but between those peaks, I'd sometimes go days without touching course materials. With my extracurriculars and everything else going on, I had to be flexible. I tried to establish regularity, but life kept throwing curveballs and I'd adapt rather than stick to a rigid schedule.
Q3: Usually it was a deadline looming or needing to check if there were any announcements I'd missed. Sometimes my peer support group would message about something posted on the LMS and that would prompt me to log in. I wasn't someone who checked it habitually every day—it was more reactive than proactive. Occasionally I'd feel a wave of anxiety about falling behind and that would push me to log in and see where I stood.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a natural part of my routine. I used it when I had to, but it felt more like a tool I reached for rather than a habit. The peer support intervention actually helped because my study partners would remind me about things, which indirectly got me logging in more. But even then, I'd often download what I needed and work offline instead of staying on the platform.
Q5: The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful because connecting with peers made the content feel less isolating. Reading others' perspectives helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. The lecture notes and reading materials were essential since I learn best by reading and writing. I also appreciated when resources were downloadable because I could engage with them on my own terms without needing to stay connected.
Q6: I barely watched the videos—I know there were over 197 minutes logged but honestly, I'd often have them playing while doing other things. I learn better from text, so videos felt inefficient for my style. Some of the optional supplementary materials I'd skip entirely because I was already overwhelmed trying to keep up with the core content. If something didn't seem directly relevant to assignments or exams, it got deprioritized.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. Peer discussions felt valuable because of the social element and different perspectives. If something was just busywork or repetitive, I'd lose patience quickly. My anxiety made me very selective—I needed to feel like my limited energy was being spent wisely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones got done because they had to, even when I was exhausted or anxious. With optional stuff, I'd think about doing it but then something required would take priority and the optional thing would slip away. It's not that I didn't value them—I just had to triage constantly, and optional always lost.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download readings and notes, print things out, and work from physical copies. I have notebooks filled with rewritten concepts and summaries that no system would ever see. I also used external resources—YouTube explanations, articles, even textbooks from the library. The LMS was more of a starting point than where learning actually happened.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be deep in study mode, working through printed materials and handwritten notes, but my LMS activity would show nothing. I'd review for exams using my own compiled notes rather than logging back into the platform. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with peers in person or over messaging apps, working through problems together. None of that shows up anywhere in the data.
Q11: All the time. I'd access something once, download it, and never go back to the LMS version. It was more convenient to have everything saved locally where I could annotate and organize it my way. I'd sometimes access materials on my phone during commutes and then study from downloaded versions later at home. The single access doesn't reflect the hours I spent with those materials afterward.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and everything felt overwhelming. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt like too much—seeing all the things I hadn't done would make me spiral. When my extracurricular commitments got intense, the course would slip. Surprisingly though, I'd always manage to pull through eventually because I'm resilient like that, even when things looked dire.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one—some weeks I was running on empty and the LMS was the last thing I wanted to look at. When I felt confused about course content, I'd actually avoid the LMS because it reminded me of what I didn't understand. Competing responsibilities constantly affected my engagement; I'd have to choose between the course and other commitments. My anxiety would sometimes paralyze me even when I had time available.
Q14: Yes, definitely. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be too anxious to actually absorb anything, just going through the motions. There were times I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without processing it. Being logged in and being mentally present are two very different things.
Q15: I postponed assignments even when I knew they were important, usually because my anxiety made starting feel impossible. The bigger or more complex something seemed, the more likely I was to avoid it initially. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but sometimes that tomorrow kept getting pushed. It wasn't laziness—it was genuine overwhelm that I had to work through.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver, honestly. I'd have bursts of intense LMS activity right before things were due. Without a deadline, I'd drift and prioritize other things. The structure of having due dates actually helped my anxiety in a weird way—it gave me a concrete target instead of vague expectations. But it also meant my engagement was very uneven throughout the course.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me when I actually received it. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me more likely to engage with similar activities. Critical feedback could go either way—sometimes it motivated me to improve, other times it triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid that type of work. I wished there had been more timely feedback because waiting made my anxiety worse.
Q18: The course structure influenced me quite a bit. When things were clearly laid out with explicit expectations, I could manage my anxiety better and plan accordingly. But when structure was loose or expectations were vague, I'd feel lost and my engagement would drop. The peer support aspect really helped—having that structured intervention gave me accountability and connection that the course itself didn't always provide.
Q19: Clear deadlines and downloadable resources made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks that felt overwhelming to track. The discussion forums were well-designed and encouraged participation. But the video-heavy content didn't suit my learning style, so that was a barrier. Having peer support built in was genuinely helpful for someone like me who struggles with isolation.
Q20: I think that could be someone exploring and trying to understand where everything is, or someone who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. It might also be someone who's struggling to start tasks—they're present and trying, but something is blocking them from producing output. High activity without submissions could signal someone who needs support, not someone who's slacking.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. I performed reasonably well—got a Merit—but my LMS activity was low because I did so much learning offline. My attendance was poor and my task completion wasn't great, but my actual understanding was solid. I think some students are just more efficient or have different learning approaches that don't require constant platform presence. The grades tell a different story than the logs.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone's active and what they're clicking on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't capture offline studying, peer conversations, or mental states. They don't know if someone's anxiously scrolling without absorbing anything or deeply focused for a short time. The logs see behavior but not understanding, presence but not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material and caring about understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting ideas to other things I know. For me, engagement often happens in my head or in conversations with peers, not necessarily on a platform. It's about mental investment, not just time spent or clicks made.
Q24: My anxiety and how much energy it takes to manage it while still performing—that's completely invisible. The peer support I received and gave, the late-night study sessions with friends, the notebooks full of rewritten notes. My resilience in bouncing back from rough patches doesn't show up anywhere. The emotional journey of this course, the surprise at my own capabilities, the growth—none of that appears in any log.
